title: Fried plantain
subtitle: Sweet ripe plantain fried golden in oil
excerpt: Ripe plantain sliced and shallow-fried in vegetable oil until golden and caramelised on both sides. Soft inside, slightly crisp at the edges, sweetly fragrant. The universal Caribbean side dish.
sourceNotes: Fried plantain (maduros or sweet fried plantain) appears across the Caribbean, West Africa, and Central America. The Caribbean version most commonly uses very ripe, almost black-skinned plantain, which is sweeter and softer than the green plantain used for tostones. In the UK, plantain is available in Caribbean and African food shops year-round. This recipe uses banana as a substitute where plantain is unavailable, though the flavour is milder and the texture slightly different. Note: a dedicated plantain ingredient should be added to the master ingredient table.

content.text: Fried plantain is the quick side that goes with almost everything in the Caribbean kitchen. The right plantain is fully ripe: the skin should be black or heavily speckled, and the flesh inside will be deep yellow-orange and very sweet. Green plantain fries up starchy and bland. If plantain is not available, very ripe banana works and gives a sweeter, softer result.

Diagonal slices fry faster than rounds and give more surface for caramelisation. Keep the heat moderate: the outside should colour slowly while the inside warms through. Too hot and the surface browns before the centre softens.

items.name: Banana
items.prepNote: ripe plantain, use very ripe (black-skinned) plantain if available, or ripe banana as a substitute

items.name: Vegetable oil
items.prepNote: about 5 mm depth in the pan

items.name: Fine sea salt
items.prepNote: optional, omit for a sweeter result

content.text: Peel the plantain or banana and cut on the diagonal into slices about 1 cm thick.
Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide frying pan over medium heat. When the oil shimmers, add the slices in a single layer.
Fry for 2 to 3 minutes until the underside is golden-brown and caramelised. Turn with tongs and fry the other side for another 2 minutes.

Lift the slices onto kitchen paper to drain for 30 seconds. A pinch of salt over the top brings out the sweetness. Serve straight away: fried plantain loses the contrast between its caramelised outside and soft inside as it cools.

items.fix: Lower the heat to medium-low. The oil should be shimmering, not smoking
items.cause: Oil too hot; very ripe plantain has a high sugar content that colours fast

items.fix: Use plantain or banana that is black-skinned but still firm to the touch. Cut slices at least 8 mm thick
items.cause: Overripe to the point of being mushy; slices too thin
items.symptom: Plantain falling apart in the pan

items.fix: Use the ripest possible banana, with brown spots throughout the skin. Plantain from a Caribbean grocer will give a more robust flavour and firmer texture than banana
items.cause: Not ripe enough; banana is a milder substitute for plantain
items.symptom: Banana not as sweet as expected

items.fix: Use 5 mm of oil rather than deep frying. Drain on kitchen paper for at least 60 seconds before serving
items.cause: Too much oil or not drained sufficiently

content.text: Fried plantain appears across West Africa, the Caribbean, and Central and South America. In the Caribbean kitchen, it sits alongside rice and peas, jerk chicken, or ackee and saltfish as a sweet counterpoint to the spice and salt of the main dish. The version here uses the sweet, ripe type. For the crisp, savoury style, use green plantain and make tostones instead. Both use the same ingredient at different stages of ripeness and give completely different results.
